# Back to the serial interval

## The event-time hierarchy

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
- A transmission pair has latent infection and transmission events
- We only directly observe symptom onsets $S_1$ and $S_2$

$$ GT = LP + U $$
$$ SI = GT + (IP_2 - IP_1) $$

- The serial interval is not a primitive event time; it is derived
- Onset timing in the infector can sit after transmission, so the
  serial interval can be negative
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figures/fig-gt-vs-si-timeline.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

# A within-host informed approach

## The idea: one trajectory, coupled delays

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
- A shared pathogen load $V(t)$ in the infector drives three event
  hazards
  - infectiousness onset $h_I$
  - symptom onset $h_S$
  - transmission $p_{\text{trans}}$
- So $LP$, $U$, and $IP_1$ are dependent, not separate marginals

$$ SI = LP + U + IP_2 - IP_1 $$

- The delays stay mutually consistent, and parameters keep a
  biological meaning: growth $r$, peak time $m$, clearance $d$
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figures/fig-event-time-dag.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

## A bit of the maths

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Rise-and-fall pathogen load: early growth $r$, peak at $m$,
  decay $d$, transition sharpness $\kappa$

$$
V(t) = A\, e^{rt}
\left\{1 + \tfrac{r}{d}\,e^{\kappa(t-m)}\right\}^{-(r+d)/\kappa}
$$

- Each event is a stochastic activation with a hazard set by load

$$
F_Z(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left\{-\!\int_0^t h_Z(s)\,ds\right\},
\quad h_Z(t) = \beta_Z\, V(t)
$$

- All four delays fall out of the same $V(t)$; simple hazard limits
  recover exponential, Weibull, and gamma shapes
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/pathogen_load_varying_kappa_peak_at_m.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper). Trajectory form follows
[Ke et al. 2021](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2111477118),
[Puhach et al. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41579-022-00822-w)
:::
:::
::::

# Results

## SARS-CoV-2: Delta to Omicron

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Dutch household transmission pairs, Delta replaced by Omicron
- Shorter mean generation time for Omicron (~3.1 d) than
  Delta (~4.0 d)
- Lower WAIC and LOOIC than a lognormal generation-time model
  for both strains
- Variation is allocated to interpretable components, so the
  generation-time spread is more constrained
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/figure3_generation_mean_sd_lognormal_vs_updatedV_obspair.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper). Data:
[Backer et al. 2022](https://doi.org/10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2022.27.6.2200042),
[Park et al. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2221887120)
:::
:::
::::

## SARS-CoV-2: fit to the serial intervals

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Both models reproduce the modal interval and the tails
- For Omicron the within-host-informed model captures the peak
  and both tails better than the lognormal
- The fit stays comparable while keeping a biological link to the
  latent generation time
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/figure_serial_fit_overlay_lognormal_vs_WHinformed_omicron_delta.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

## Mpox: bringing in external viral-load information

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- 34 high-confidence mpox transmission pairs
- External viral-load data (Yang et al.) build informative priors
  on the trajectory parameters $(\log r, \log d, m, \log\kappa)$
- Transmission-pair timing alone does not pin down the within-host
  trajectory: $\log d$ and $m$ shift under the Yang prior
- External information constrains weakly identified parameters,
  rather than just improving the serial-interval fit
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/WH_prior_posterior_comparison_logr_logd_logkappa_m.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host-informed event-time framework (Abbott et al.,
working paper). Mpox pairs:
[Miura et al. 2024](https://doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jiad091);
viral load:
[Yang et al. 2024](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-48754-8)
:::
:::
::::

# You don't need a within-host model

## The hierarchy is agnostic to the hazard

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- The event-time hierarchy holds for any hazard form; $V(t)$ is one
  choice
- A generic alternative: contact-interval survival models
  ([Kenah 2011](https://doi.org/10.1093/biostatistics/kxq068))
- Time from infectiousness onset to infectious contact is a
  transmission hazard, with no within-host trajectory needed
- Contact intervals are right-censored, so survival analysis is the
  natural tool
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- This is the natural meeting point with survival analysis
- Household and mechanistic models give the same view of
  generation-time identifiability
  ([Hart et al. 2022](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.70767))
- Joint within-host kinetics models are an active direction
  ([Boyer et al. 2026](https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2605.20692))
- Pick the hazard to match the data and the question, not the other
  way round
  ([Charniga et al. 2024](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012520))
:::
::::
